feed.title: Combined summary - [Research] Bitcoin After Block Rewards : preventing miner's deviation when the Bitcoin rewards is zero

entry.title: Combined summary - [Research] Bitcoin After Block Rewards : preventing miner's deviation when the Bitcoin rewards is zero
entry.summary: Junhyuk Lee's paper critically examines the future of Bitcoin’s security as block rewards diminish, an issue expected to intensify by 2140. The study highlights concerns about decreasing miner profitability due to reward halving, which may not be offset by equivalent rises in Bitcoin prices. This potential reduction in earnings could lead to deviations from expected miner behaviors, posing risks to the network’s integrity. Lee introduces a threshold (G_t) to detect such deviations, suggesting that significant variations in miner behavior could occur even with transaction fees as low as 0.17% of transaction values, especially in a pure fee-only regime. The paper also explores solutions for this looming challenge, proposing the implementation of a minimum transaction fee through soft-fork compatible methods and combining tail emission with an EIP-1559 style fee burn and priority tipping. Such strategies aim to maintain network sustainability by balancing transaction fee dynamics. For those interested in a deeper exploration of these concepts, the original content is available [here](https://delvingbitcoin.org/t/addressing-the-diminishing-block-subsidy/2640). Feedback on Lee's paper points out its verbosity and suggests simplification to make the analysis more accessible. The critique emphasizes the importance of practical revisions, including the reevaluation of static mining models that don't account for fluctuating economic conditions and strategic miner behaviors. It proposes integrating dynamic security models that consider economic and community trust factors, enhancing the blockchain’s resilience against threats like Sybil attacks. Cameron’s response to the paper underscores the unrealistic assumptions in the game theory model used, advocating for a more holistic approach that accounts for varying mining capacities and the broader stake of the system in maintaining security. He suggests revising the theoretical framework to include dynamic elements that reflect more accurately the complex interplay of economic forces and social trust within blockchain operations. In conclusion, while Junhyuk’s paper sets a foundation for discussing Bitcoin’s post-subsidy challenges, it invites further research and refinement to develop robust, dynamic models that ensure the long-term integrity and security of blockchain networks. This ongoing discussion highlights the need for adaptive strategies that address both the economic incentives and the broader trust mechanisms essential to blockchain technology.
